coredata.dc:title: Pulmonary microbiota disruption by respiratory exposure to carbon quantum dots induces neuronal damages in mice
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Tianshu
Liu, Kehan
Chen, Siyuan
Ye, Zongjian
Xia, Jieyi
He, Jing
Xing, Pengcheng
Yang, Jiafu
Qian, Yijing
Chen, Min
coredata.dc:description: Given the fact that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been commercially produced in quantities, it is inevitable to make their ways into environment and interact closely with the public. Even though CQDs in the environment have been reported to damage the central nervous system, the underlying mechanisms of neurotoxic effects of CQDs following respiratory exposure is still not clear. Intranasal instilled CQDs, mimicking respiratory exposure, induces neurobehavioral impairments associated with neuronal cell death of ferroptosis and disulfidptosis that is regulated by metabolic reprogramming of glutathione and cysteine pathways in the cortex and hippocampus where CQDs were hardly accumulated. Therefore, further exploration found that dysbiosis in the lung microbiome was found specifically manipulated by CQDs, which correlated with systemic and neuroinflammatory responses, implicating a lung-brain axis other than gut-brain axis as a critical pathway through which microbiota dysbiosis may impact neurological health after respiratory exposure to CQDs. This study pioneers the exploration of the neurological consequences of inhaled CQDs in the environment through the regulation of microbiome-lung-brain axis, which is key in understanding the mechanistic link between CQDs exposure and neurotoxicity. The findings could develop potential strategies for mitigating the neurological effects of CQDs even other types of nanoparticles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Neurobehavioral impairment
Ferroptosis
Disulfidptosis
Glutathione and cysteine reprogramming
Lung-brain axis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: 16 s rRNA sequencing of BALF and feces
Measurements of inflammatory cytokines, ferrous iron, cysteine and lipid peroxidation
Physiochemical characteristics of CQDs and their accumulation in multiple organs of mice
Intranasal administration of CQDs caused behavioral impairments attributed to neuronal deaths in mice
CQDs caused cysteine and glutathione metabolic reprogramming to trigger neuronal ferroptosis and disulfidptosis in brain of mice
CQDs remarkably induced pulmonary rather than intestinal microbial dysbiosis in mice

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The physicochemical characterization of CQDs. (a) TEM image and HR-TEM image inserted; (b) The size distribution; (c) FT-IR spectrum; (d∼f) Full range of XPS spectrum (d) along with high resolution XPS spectra of C1s (e) and O1s (f); (g) Hydrous particle diameters; (h) ξ-potentials.

CQDs induced neurobehavioral injuries in mice. (a) Representative track images in the open field test, and quantitative results of parameter analysis including (b) total moved distance, (c) duration of movement, (d) time spent in center, (e) frequency to center; (f) Representative track images in the Morris water maze, and quantitative results of parameter analysis including (g) escape latency, (h) swim distance, (i) number of platform crossings, (j) time spent in target quadrant. Mice were intranasally treated with CQDs at 0.5 mg/kg BW (Low-CQDs) and 5 mg/kg BW (High-CQDs) for consecutive 28 days. Data are showed as mean + SD of three independent experiments. (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001).

CQDs triggered typical phenotypes related with ferroptosis and disulfidptosis in brain of mice. (a, b) Representative histological images represented the brain tissue stained with Nissl staining, Scale bar: 100 μm and 20 μm, and quantitative analysis; (c∼e) Representative fluorescent images of NeuN expression (red), TUNEL staining (green) and DAPI staining (blue) in brain tissue, Scale bar: 100 μm and 20 μm, and quantitative analysis; (f) Levels of ferrous iron; (g) MDA contents; (h) Non-reducing and reducing WB analysis of actin cytoskeleton protein Actin. Mice were intranasally treated with CQDs at 0.5 mg/kg BW (Low-CQDs) and 5 mg/kg BW (High-CQDs) for consecutive 28 days. Data are showed as mean + SD of three independent experiments. (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001).

In situ visualization of critical metabolites in glutathione and cysteine metabolic pathways altered by CQDs in brain of mice. (a) Bubble chart for the enrichment analysis of TOP20 metabolic pathways based on all metabolites significantly changed by CQDs in cortex and hippocampus; (b, c, e) MS images and levels of glutathione (GSH) (b), oxidized glutathione (GSSG) (c) and Cysteine (e) in different regions of brain; (d) GSH/GSSG ratio and (f) Cysteine contents in cortex and hippocampus. Mice were intranasally treated with CQDs at 0.5 mg/kg BW (Low-CQDs) and 5 mg/kg BW (High-CQDs) for consecutive 28 days. Whole brain (WB), Olfactory bulb (OB), Cortex (COR), Hippocampus (HIP), Corpus Striatum (CS), Midbrain (MB) and Cerebellum (CE). Data are showed as mean + SD of three independent experiments. (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001).

CQDs affected critical regulators of cysteine and glutathione metabolic pathways in brain of mice. (a) Glutathione and cysteine metabolic process; (b∼d) Representative IHC staining showed the expression of SLC7A11 and IHC scores; (e∼g) Representative IHC staining showed the expression of NADPH and IHC scores; (h) NADP+/NADPH ratio; (i∼k) Representative IHC staining showed the expression of GPx4 and IHC scores; (l, m) Expression of SLC7A11 and GPx4 proteins levels, and quantitative analysis. Mice were intranasally treated with CQDs at 0.5 mg/kg BW (Low-CQDs) and 5 mg/kg BW (High-CQDs) for consecutive 28 days. Data are showed as mean + SD of three independent experiments. (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001).

Features of pulmonary microbiome in CQDs exposed mice. (a) Absolute abundance of microbiota; (b∼f) Bacterial taxonomic profiles at the phylum (b), class (c), order (d), family (e) and genus (f) levels; (g) Correlations between different microbial species at phylum level and sample characteristics using the circus analysis to identify clusters of samples that share similar microbial compositions; (h) Ternary Plot of relative abundance of microbiota among samples to indicate the proportions of three most dominant microbial taxa in samples; (i) Phylogenetic tree showing the evolutionary relationships among bacteria at phylum level to the genetic distances and relationships among different phyla; (j) Cladogram of bacteria using the linear discriminant analysis (LDA) coupled with effect size (LEfSe) to identify bacterial taxa with significant differential abundance among samples. Mice were intranasally treated with CQDs at 0.5 mg/kg BW (Low-CQDs) and 5 mg/kg BW (High-CQDs) for consecutive 28 days.

CQDs caused the disturbance of lung microbiota in mice. (a) Venn diagram of significantly different bacterial abundance to illustrate the unique and shared significantly different bacterial species across different samples; (b∼f) Index of Ace and Chao (b), Simpson and Shannon (c), PD whole tree (d), observed species (e) and goods coverage (f) in α-diversity analysis; (g) The dissimilarities in microbial composition on OUT level determined by principal coordinates analysis (PCoA) and distance between points indicates the degree of dissimilarity in microbial composition; (h∼j) Significant differences on the community abundance at family (h), genus (i) and species (j) levels; (k) Score map of bacterial clades and biologically consistent difference to identify the biological consistent different between bacterial clades across the different groups; (l) Co-occurrence network of microbial communities, nodes represent species and edges indicate the microbial interactions and potential ecological networks; (m) Map of microbiota importance points using randomForest analysis to identify the most influential features including species and OTUs in predicting the microbial community structure. Mice were intranasally treated with CQDs at 0.5 mg/kg BW (Low-CQDs) and 5 mg/kg BW (High-CQDs) for consecutive 28 days. Data are showed as mean + SD of three independent experiments. (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001).

CQDs caused inflammatory reactions in lung, brain and circulation system of mice. (a∼c) Representative histological images represented the cortex, hippocampus and lung tissues stained with H&E staining, as well as inflammatory infiltration areas and scores; (d) Representative IHC images of IBA1 expressions in hippocampus with skeletonize and sholl analysis; (e) The number of microglia; (f∼i) soma size (f), junctions (g), endpoints (h) and process lengths (i) of microglia; (j, k) Immune cell profile and quantitative analysis in BLAF; (l∼n) IL-1ß (l), TNF-α (m) and IL-6 (n) contents in cortex, hippocampus, lung and serum. Mice were intranasally treated with CQDs at 0.5 mg/kg BW (Low-CQDs) and 5 mg/kg BW (High-CQDs) for consecutive 28 days. Data are showed as mean + SD of three independent experiments. (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001).

Altered lung microbiota correlated with neuroinflammation in mice treated with CQDs. (a) Top 15 altered functional abundance of microbial community using the clusters of orthologous groups (COGs) analysis; (b) Top 15 altered functional abundance of microbial community using Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) Orthology (KO) analysis; (c) Average abundance of Top 15 KEGG pathways; Correlation between lung microbiota alterations and inflammatory indices in lung (d), circulation system (e), cortex (f) and hippocampus (g). The correlation coefficients (r and P value) were obtained from Pearson correlation analysis, and a red color indicates a positive relationship while a blue color indicates a negative relationship. Mice were intranasally treated with CQDs at 0.5 mg/kg BW (Low-CQDs) and 5 mg/kg BW (High-CQDs) for consecutive 28 days. (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001).

head.ce:title: Pulmonary microbiota disruption by respiratory exposure to carbon quantum dots induces neuronal damages in mice

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education, Nanjing 210009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education; School of Public Health, Southeast University, Nanjing 210009, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Public Health, Southeast University, Nanjing 210009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Public Health, Southeast University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education; School of Public Health, Southeast University, Nanjing 210009, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yancheng Kindergarten Teachers College, Yancheng 224005, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yancheng Kindergarten Teachers College, Yancheng 224005, China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Key Laboratory of Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education, School of Public Health, Southeast University, Nanjing 210009, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education, School of Public Health, Southeast University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Given the fact that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been commercially produced in quantities, it is inevitable to make their ways into environment and interact closely with the public. Even though CQDs in the environment have been reported to damage the central nervous system, the underlying mechanisms of neurotoxic effects of CQDs following respiratory exposure is still not clear. Intranasal instilled CQDs, mimicking respiratory exposure, induces neurobehavioral impairments associated with neuronal cell death of ferroptosis and disulfidptosis that is regulated by metabolic reprogramming of glutathione and cysteine pathways in the cortex and hippocampus where CQDs were hardly accumulated. Therefore, further exploration found that dysbiosis in the lung microbiome was found specifically manipulated by CQDs, which correlated with systemic and neuroinflammatory responses, implicating a lung-brain axis other than gut-brain axis as a critical pathway through which microbiota dysbiosis may impact neurological health after respiratory exposure to CQDs. This study pioneers the exploration of the neurological consequences of inhaled CQDs in the environment through the regulation of microbiome-lung-brain axis, which is key in understanding the mechanistic link between CQDs exposure and neurotoxicity. The findings could develop potential strategies for mitigating the neurological effects of CQDs even other types of nanoparticles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a class of zero-dimensional carbon-based luminescent nanoparticles and have emerged as innovative materials with widely applications because of their unique characteristics [1–3]. However, the increasing applications of CQDs, especially their outstanding environmental applications, raise concerns about their environmental exposure and potential health impacts [4–6]. Notably, the environmental burden of CQDs could enter living organisms and poses a high risk to ecosystems and human health. Recent studies have demonstrated the adverse biological effects of CQDs, especially under respiratory exposure that is considered a primary route of entry into the body [7]. Particularly, inhaled CQDs could access the central nervous system (CNS) through the blood-brain barrier (BBB) or the olfactory nerve pathway [1,8], which raises the concern of their bio-safety in the CNS. Therefore, it is urgent and important to elucidate the neurotoxicity of pulmonary exposed CQDs.

Recently, our previous study indicated that intranasal exposure to CQDs can induce multiple injuries associated with oxidative stress, neuroinflammation and neuronal death, leading to neurobehavioral impairments [9]. Similar neurotoxicity of CQDs has been reported by a growing number of researches [10–13], but a comprehensive understanding of how CQDs inducing neurotoxicity, especially following with respiratory exposure, remains elusive. Recently, the role of respiratory tract microbiota in regulating neuronal functions and neurobehavior has been gained increasing attention, so more and more researchers tried to figure out the complex relationship. Studies have indicated that the lung microbiome that was once considered sterile is actually colonized by various microorganisms and capable of not only influencing the development and progression of respiratory system diseases but also playing an important role in the modulation of several neurological disorders [14,15].

Microbiome in the body, especially resident in lung and gut have been considered as pivotal players in the environment-health interaction and should be taken into account when conducting risk assessments of environmental pollutants. Therefore, recent studies have tried to demonstrate the pathway of nanoparticles in the environment interacting with the microbiota at a molecular level and their downstream effects, but focusing on gut microbiome [16,17]. Since respiratory exposure is one of main exposed ways for nanoparticles in the environment, it is validly to investigate how the inhaled nanoparticles might affect the lung microbiota and consequently lead to neurotoxicity. Available studies showed that the respiratory microbiota could affect the nervous system through multiple ways including direct microbial translocation, neuromodulation, hormonal regulation, and immune modulation [14,15]. Nanoparticles exposed through respiration has a high probability of interacting with microorganisms in the lung, which is a primary target organ of inhaled environmental contaminants, due to their small size and large surface area [18]. Thus, the lung microbiome could play an important role in neurotoxicity of nanoparticles, but the investigation of neurotoxic effects of CQDs focusing on the manipulation of respiratory microbiota is still at its early stage. This study tried to explore the mechanisms by which intranasal treated CQDs, mimicking respiratory exposure, disrupted the delicate balance of respiratory microbiota and consequently impaired the CNS, which introduces a new dimension to our understanding of environmental health risks. The finding is an insight that could lead to the development of strategies to mitigate the potential neurological risks of not only CQDs but also other nanoparticles.

DAPI dye, GSH/GSSG Assay Kit, NADP+/NADPH Assay Kit and Lipid Peroxidation MDA Assay Kit were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology (Shanghai, China). Cysteine Content Test Kit was purchased from Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute (Nanjing, China). Ferrous Iron Assay Kit was purchased from Shanghai solarbio Bioscience & Technology Co.,LTD (Shanghai, China). IL-1ß, TNF-α and IL-6 Commercial Reagent Kits were purchased from Yi Fei Xue Biotechnology (Nanjing, China). NAPDH, GPx4, SLC7A11, actin, and GAPDH primary antibodies, as well as secondary antibodies of HRP conjugated anti-rabbit or anti-mouse IgG were all purchased from ABclonal Technology (Wuhan, China).

CQDs (Product No. XF253) used here was purchased from XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Before conducting experiments, their physicochemical characterizations were evaluated. A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL Ltd. Japan) was used to measure the structure of CQDs. The fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra recorded on Nicolet iS10 spectrometer (Thermo, USA) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250, Thermo Electron Corporation, USA) were used to measure functional groups and chemical states at the surface of CQDs. A UV-2550 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used to measure the absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of CQDs. A Malcern Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (Zetasizer Nano-ZS90, Malvern, UK) was used to measure the dynamic light scattering (DLS) and surface ξ-potential.

Total 33 male 8 weeks C57BL/6 mice (11 mice per group) were purchased from Zhejiang Academy of Medical Sciences (Zhejiang, China). All mice were housed in stainless steel cages in a ventilated animal facility at a temperature maintained in 22+ 2 °C and relative humidity of 65+ 10 % under a 12 h light/dark cycle, and feed with sterilized food and distilled water. All mice were treated humanely throughout the experimental period, and procedures were performed in strict accordance with the Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of Southeast University. Experiments used in this study were approved by the Animal Experimental Ethics Committee of Southeast University (Nanjing, China).

The treated mice were intranasally instilled with 0.5 and 5 mg/kg BW CQDs (approximately 10 µl per nose for each mouse) for 28 consecutive days, while the control mice were provided normal saline following with the same administration. Before animal treatment, CQDs were dissolved in normal saline and sonicated for 30 min every day using an ultrasonic instrument (KH600DB, Hechuang Ultrasonic Instrument Co., LTD, Kunshan, China) to ensure CQDs evenly dispersing in normal saline. At the end of treatment, mice were sacrificed by inhaling carbon dioxide and then quickly removed and weighted their main organs after behavioral experiments. Blood samples were harvested to evaluate routine blood cells and serum biochemical indexes. Brains or hippocampus, lungs, intestines and livers were dissected and fixed in 4 % (w/v) paraformaldehyde or stored in liquid nitrogen.

Each mouse was carefully positioned at the heart of a quadrilateral arena, measuring 40 centimeters by 40 centimeters, and permitted to roam without restraint for a duration of 5 minutes. The locomotion of each rodent was captured using a digital camera, which was subsequently subjected to analysis via a specialized animal motion tracking apparatus, namely EthoVision XT 17.0. The resultant data from the open-field examination were delineated in terms of the aggregate distance and time traversed, as well as the interval of time and frequency the rodent spent in the central zone.

Morris water maze procedure was initiated subsequent to the mice. The cylindrical pool was sectioned into four symmetrical sectors and filled to capacity with water. Initially, the mice were introduced to the water maze to acclimate, engaging in a 60 second unrestricted swim without the presence of a platform, prior to the commencement of the trials. A test involving a submerged platform was executed over a span of four successive days. At the concluding day, the submerged platform was removed for a post-training exploratory test. The trajectory of each mouse's swim was documented using a camera suspended above the pool. Metrics including the time taken to reach the platform (escape latency), the total distance swim, number of platform crossings, and time spent in target quadrant were all extracted from the motion tracking system (EthoVision XT 17.0).

Tissue slices of brain, lung, intestine, and liver were stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E). Photographic documentation was captured across five distinct, non-adjacent, and randomly chosen areas within hippocampus and cortex, pulmonary bronchus and alveoli, small intestine and colon, and hepatic lobule from each section of brain, lung, intestine and liver tissues using a microscope. The photographs were employed to determine the inflammatory infiltration area and grading for each experimental cohort. The grading was ascertained by evaluating the proportional area occupied by inflammatory cells within every tissue section, and that is categorized as follows: no presence (0), up to 5 % (1), up to 10 % (2), up to 20 % (3), and more than 20 % (4). The TUNEL assay was implemented for the identification of cells undergoing apoptosis and those that had perished within the brain. The intestine tissues were stained with periodic acid Schiff (PAS) to assess glycogen and other polysaccharides, and neutral mucus in colonic goblet cells.

Slices of brain were initially cleaned with a 0.1 M TBS solution, followed by a 30 min pre-treatment with 3 % hydrogen peroxide to inactivate intrinsic peroxidase activity. After rinsing, they were soaked in a 3 % FBS-TBS blocking solution for another 30 min at room temperature. The sections were then exposed to primary antibodies targeting IBA1, NADPH, GPx4 and SLC7A11 at a 1:100 dilution, and then incubated overnight at 4 °C. Subsequently, the slices were treated with a biotinylated anti-rabbit or anti-mouse IgG secondary antibody at a 1:500 dilution, followed by DAB staining to reveal the signals. The sections were examined under a microscope, and photographs were captured. The images were processed using the IHC profiler in ImageJ software (version 1.53) to determine the IHC positive area and IHC score, which were assigned based on ten randomly selected non-overlapping fields and categorized as follows: High Positive (4), Positive (3), Low Positive (2), and Negative (1). Additionally, The Sholl analysis used to measure the number of microglia, and soma size, junctions, endpoints, process lengths of microglia using ImageJ software under IBA1 IHC staining. The assessment was conducted on 5 distinct areas per sections, and the procedure was overseen by a seasoned pathologist who was unaware of the experimental setup.

The cryo-preserved brain tissues were treated with primary antibody specific for NeuN diluted to 1:300 and incubated at 4 °C overnight. Subsequently, the sections were treated with a Cy3-tagged anti-mouse IgG secondary antibody at a 1:500 dilution for 1 hour at room temperature and then TUNEL dye. Examination and photography of sections were performed using a confocal microscope. The colocalization analysis was conducted using ImageJ software and the Coloc2 plugin.

Approximately 1 mL chilled and sterile phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was administered into the pulmonary system of each mouse through the trachea using a syringe, a process repeated thrice to procure roughly 3 mL BALF. The immune cells in BALF were measured using a microscope (AXIO Imager M2, ZEISS, Germany). The BALF was then subjected to centrifugation at a rotation speed of 600 revolutions per minute (RPM) for 5 min at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 16 s rRNA sequencing of BALF and feces
ce:section.ce:para: BALF (low biomass specimens) were collected prior to feces (high biomass specimens) for 16 s rRNA sequencing. Total genomic DNA in BALF and feces was extracted after pulverizing at −80 °C using MagPure Soil DNA LQ kit (Magan) according to the manufacturer’s protocol. The extracted DNA served as a template for PCR to amplify bacterial 16S rRNA genes sequences, utilizing barcoded primers on a Takara Ex Taq (Takara). Subsequent sequencing was carried out on an Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform, which generated 250 bp paired-end reads. The sequencing service was provided by OE Biotech Company (Shanghai, China). The raw FASTQ data were preprocessed with cutadapt to remove adapters, followed by quality trimming, denoising, read merging, and chimeric sequence identification using DADA2 within QIIME2. The process culminated in the generation of a table detailing representative ASV reads and their abundances. These reads were annotated against the Silva database Version 138 (16 s rDNA) using QIIME2's q2-feature-classifier with default parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of inflammatory cytokines, ferrous iron, cysteine and lipid peroxidation
ce:section.ce:para: Levels of IL-1ß, TNF-α, IL-6, MDA, ferrous iron, and cysteine, as well as ratios of NADP+/NADPH and GSH/GSSG in the supernatants of lysis of hippocampus, cortex, lung or intestine were measured using commercial reagent kits based on the manufacturer’s instructions. Samples were performed in triplicate in three independent experiments.

The quantification of protein expression across various brain regions was conducted through the technique of western blotting. Proteins were extracted from the tissues using a Radio-Immunoprecipitation Assay (RIPA) buffer, which was enhanced with a 1 % concentration of a protease inhibitor mixture sourced from Millipore, USA. The protein concentration was subsequently quantified employing the Bicinchoninic Acid (BCA) assay kit, procured from Beyotime (Shanghai, China). The proteins were electrophoresized in a 10 % sodium dodecyl sulfatepolyacrylamide gelelectrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) separation gel, transferred to PVDF membranes and then blocked in 5 % non-fat milk at room temperature for 1 h. After that, membranes were washed with Tris buffered saline tween (TBST) and then incubated with primary antibodies at 4 oC overnight. After washed with TBST, membranes were subsequently incubated with secondary antibodies at room temperature for 2 h. Finally, enhanced chemiluminenscence (ECL) solution (Millipore, USA) was used to exhibit protein bends. The densitometry analysis of WB results was using ImageJ software to get grey values of each bend. All protein levels were normalized to reference protein GAPDH. The same samples ran three times and the experiment was repeated independently at least three times.

All data were displayed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD). Statistical analysis was performed using Graphpad Prism 9.0 Software. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to determine the statistical significance among groups followed with the Dunnett’s t test to determine the significance of differences between treatment groups and the control. Pearson correlation analysis was used to determine the correlation among multiple indices. Probability levels of < 0.05, < 0.01 and < 0.001 were considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physiochemical characteristics of CQDs and their accumulation in multiple organs of mice
ce:section.ce:para: The physicochemical properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are crucial prior to evaluating their potential risks in biological systems. Observed results from HR-TEM revealed that CQDs exhibited a spherical morphology and were uniformly distributed with a medium diameter of 2.91 nm ( Fig. 1a). Meanwhile, the measured average interplanar distance of 0.21 nm corresponding to the lattice spacing of (100) planes in graphite (the inserted image in Fig. 1a) suggested a graphitic core structure of CQDs [12,19]. FT-IR spectroscopy detected the presence of O-H stretching vibrations at approximately 3301 cm−1 and CO vibrations at around 1630 cm−1 (Fig. 1d). Similar to FT-IR results, the analysis of XPS peak differentiation imitating on the high resolution XPS spectra of C1s and O1s indicated the presence of C-C, C-OH, CO, O-H, and C-O (Fig. 1d∼1 f). The abundant hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of CQDs ensure them disperse well in aqueous environment, including biological medium. Hydrodynamic diameter of CQDs in saline solution was around 430.6 nm, which slightly exceeds the size observed by TEM (Fig. 1g). Zeta potential of CQDs was negative round −36.3 mV (Fig. 1h). Although a surface charge below −30 mV on nanomaterials typically prevents aggregation due to strong electrostatic repulsion, the aggregation of CQDs could be attribute to factors such as neutralization of charge by specific ions or molecules, or the overcoming of electrostatic forces by van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding [20]. Details regarding the physicochemical properties of the CQDs are also presented in Supplementary Table S1.

Due to the excellent fluorescent properties of CQDs (Figure S1a), we can directly observe the distribution and accumulation of CQDs after respiratory exposure to mice. Firstly, the optical properties of CQDs used in this study were measured. The UV−Vis spectrum showed a common absorption band for CQDs in the region of 226–240 nm ascribed to the π−π* transition of the C-C and CC bonds in the aromatic sp
2 domains of the core, while the other slight absorption peak at 300 nm originate from the n − π* transition of multi-conjugate CO and C-O groups on the surface of CQDs (Figure S1b) [12,21]. When recording the photoluminescence (PL) of CQDs, the emission spectra were analyzed at different excitation wavelengths increasing from 360 nm to 400 nm at a 20 nm increment, and the emission peaks of CQDs were red-shifted from 455 nm to 495 nm (Figure S1c). Therefore, under the excitation wavelength of 380 nm, the radiant efficiencies of multiple organs and different regions of brain showed that intranasally administrative CQDs might accumulated in lung and intestine, as well as cortex of brain at a dose-dependent manner (Figure S1d∼S1g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intranasal administration of CQDs caused behavioral impairments attributed to neuronal deaths in mice
ce:section.ce:para: During the exposure to CQDs for consecutive 28 days, no individual death and severe damages on major organs of mice were observed (Figure S2a and S2b). At the end of exposure duration, the open field test was firstly used to evaluate the locomotion and spontaneous activity of mice treated with CQDs. Tracks of mice in open fields showcased the movement patterns altered by CQDs ( Fig. 2a), which are than quantitatively analyzed. Apart from parameters of total moved distance and time spent in the central area of open field, mice treated with 5 mg/kg BW CQDs significantly decreased the total time spent moving and how often the mice enter the center, which indicated that CQDs might damage general locomotor activity and induce anxiety-like behavior (Fig. 2b∼2e).

Additionally, the Morris water maze experiment was utilized to evaluate the spatial learning capabilities and memory performance of mice exposed to CQDs. When quantitively analyzing swimming tracks of mice (Fig. 2f), even though the time taken by mice to find the hidden platform did not change after CQDs exposure, the total distance swum by mice to find the platform significantly increased while the times and duration of mice crossing the platform and its located quadrant significantly decreased when the exposure concentration of CQDs reached to 5 mg/kg BW (Fig. 2g∼2j). The results reflected the impairments on efficiency of mice in navigating the maze and mice’s memory of the platform’s location.

As we known, changes in locomotor activity, anxiety-like behavior, and spatial learning and memory are highly related with damages on neurons. Firstly, the number of neurons stained with Nissl in hippocampus of mice treated with high-dose CQDs significantly decreased than the control ( Fig. 3a and b). Additionally, the number of dead neuronal cells marked by NeuN and TUNEL significantly increased in cortex and hippocampus of mice exposed to high-dose CQDs (Fig. 3c∼e). Therefore, it seems like the neuronal injury in brain regions of cortex and hippocampus caused by CQDs could contribute to neurobehavioral deficits of mice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs caused cysteine and glutathione metabolic reprogramming to trigger neuronal ferroptosis and disulfidptosis in brain of mice
ce:section.ce:para: Our previous studies suggested that CQDs exposure could trigger multiple types of cell death in brain of mice [9], especially ferroptosis that has been found in brain of mice exposed to carbon-based QDs [22–24]. Thus, we firstly assessed some typical phenotypes of ferroptosis, i.e ferrous iron overload and lipid peroxidation. The results found that CQDs enhanced both levels of ferrous iron and MDA contents in cortex and hippocampus of mice at a dose-dependent manner (Fig. 3f and g).

In order to investigate the potential mechanisms of CQDs inducing ferroptotic alterations in specific regions, i.e. cortex and hippocampus, of brain of mice, we used a novel technique called airflow-assisted desorption electrospray ionization mass spectrometry imaging (AFADESI-MSI) to high-throughput discover the metabolites altered by CQDs in the native brain state of mice. The metabolites significantly changed by 5 mg/kg BW CQDs in cortex and hippocampus of brain were explored enriching in the metabolic pathway of glutathione (GSH) ( Fig. 4a), which has been reported to be involved initiating ferroptosis [25–27]. The MSI images of GSH elicited the ion intensities were significantly lower, but oxidized glutathione (GSSG) significantly up-regulated than the control in the cortex and hippocampus after CQDs exposure (Fig. 4b and c). Additionally, the significantly reductions on GSH/GSSG ration in these two brain regions also confirmed the GSH depletion (Fig. 4d), which is widely recognized main cause of ferroptosis [25–27]. When assessing the upstream of GSH, we found the content of cysteine, as an essential precursor in the biosynthesis process of GSH, were also significantly lower than the control in cortex and hippocampus of brain (Fig. 4e and f).

A recent study suggested that the cysteine metabolic reprogramming is highly related to a novel regulated cell death named by disulfidptosis, and the protein disulfide-bond formation in actin cytoskeleton proteins was one of typical alterations [28]. Since disulfide-bond formation is known to affect the electrophoretic mobility of proteins under non-reducing conditions, so non-reducing western blotting of cytoskeleton protein Actin was conducted. Under non-reducing conditions, we observed obviously slower migration with smears and high-molecular-weight bands near the stacking layer in Actin of cortex and hippocampus of mice treated with high-dose CQDs, but no same migration patterns were showed under reducing conditions even after CQDs exposure (Fig. 3h), which indicated that inhaled CQDs was able to induce actin formed multiple intermolecular disulfide bonds in neuronal cells of hippocampus and cortex.

In the cysteine and glutathione metabolic pathway ( Fig. 5a), some proteins and coenzymes play critical roles, including solute carrier family 7 member 11 (SLC7A11), nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate, reduced form (NADPH), and glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPx4). SLC7A11 is located on the cell membrane and is responsible for transporting cystine from outside the cell to the inside [25]. NADPH is an important coenzyme that participates in a variety of redox reactions within the cell. In the biosynthesis of cysteine, NADPH provides reducing power to cystine reductase, that reduced the disulfide bond in cystine to two thiol groups, thus synthesizing cysteine [29]. In glutathione metabolism, NADPH serves as a coenzyme for glutathione reductase (GR), which uses NADPH to reduce oxidized GSSG back to its reduced form GSH. GPx4 is an antioxidant enzyme that uses GSH as a reducing agent to eliminate peroxides within the cell [25]. The results suggested that the expressions of SLA711 up-regulated in cortex and hippocampus of mice after CQDs exposure (Fig. 5b∼5d, l, 5m), which might lead to increasing uptake of cystine. However, the decreased expressions of NADPH and increased NADP+/NADPH ratio in cortex and hippocampus of mice treated with CQDs both indicated NADPH depletion that could attribute to CQDs-induced disulfide-bond formation in actin and GSH depletion (Fig. 5e∼h). Meanwhile, the down-regulated expression of GPx4 caused by CQDs exposure subsequently resulting in lipid peroxidation (Fig. 5i∼m). All in all, CQDs affected critical enzymes to reprogram cysteine and glutathione metabolic pathways that involved in regulating neuronal ferroptosis and disulfidptosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs remarkably induced pulmonary rather than intestinal microbial dysbiosis in mice
ce:section.ce:para: A dysregulation in the lung microbiome have been found to remarkably influence functions of the central nervous system [14]. Therefore, we conducted a comprehensively analysis of the pulmonary microbiome in mice exposed to varying concentrations of CQDs for a period of 28 days through intranasal treatment, which might highly be related with above neuronal damages. Alterations in the pulmonary microbial load suggested that CQDs modulated the overall bacterial population in the lungs ( Fig. 6a). Meanwhile, distinct distributions of bacterial communities at levels of phylum, class, order, family and genus, particularly after higher dosed CQDs exposure provided a more refined view of the microbial shifts (Fig. 6b∼f). The circus analysis revealed distinct patterns of co-occurrence and exclusion among bacterial taxa, highlighting the complex interactions within the pulmonary microbiota in response to CQDs exposure (Fig. 6g). The ternary plot provided a clear depiction of the microbial distribution across CQDs-exposure by visually representing the relative abundance of microbiota, which facilitates the comparison of microbial composition and its variation due to CQDs (Fig. 6h). Moreover, the phylogenetic tree showing the evolutionary relationships among bacteria at the phylum level offered insights into the taxonomic diversity and evolutionary divergence within the pulmonary microbiome of CQDs-exposed mice (Fig. 6i). Finally, a cladogram was generated to highlight the differentially abundant bacterial taxa associated with CQDs exposure, which could provide potential biomarkers for CQDs-induced pulmonary microbiome alterations (Fig. 6j).

Based on the features of microbiome in lungs of mice treated with/without CQDs, the alterations in microbial diversity and community structure at various taxonomic levels after CQDs exposure were evaluated. The overlap and uniqueness of bacterial taxa showed significant differences in abundance among low- and high-dose CQDs and the control, which revealed distinct microbial populations associated with varying levels of CQDs exposure ( Fig. 7a). The details in shared and unique phyla were shown in Table S2. The α-analysis results illustrated that the Ace and Chao indices, estimators of bacterial richness were significantly different from both two concentrations of CQDs to the control, suggesting that CQDs exposure may influence the complexity of microbial species present in the lungs (Fig. 7b). A significant shift in the Shannon index upon CQDs exposure implied alterations in the evenness of the lung microbiota, potentially leading to an imbalance in microbial ecosystems (Fig. 7c). However, the indices of Simpson (a measure of diversity of species distribution), PD whole tree (a metric of phylogenetic diversity), number of observed species (a direct measure of species richness), and goods coverage (an estimate of the proportion of species detected in a sample relative to the total species in the community) did not change after CQDs exposure (Fig. 7c∼7 f).

PCoA was performed to visualize the dissimilarities in microbial composition based on operational taxonomic units (OTUs), which highlighted distinct clustering patterns among control and CQDs-treated groups, indicating that CQDs exposure significantly influences the overall microbial community structure (Fig. 7g). Moreover, remarkable alterations in the community abundance of family, genus, and species in response to CQDs exposure indicated that CQDs can induce taxonomic-specific changes in the lung microbiota of mice (Fig. 7h∼j). A score map of bacterial clades highlighted the taxa that were significantly different between control and two CQDs-treated groups (Fig. 7k). The interactions among microbial communities revealed the complex relationships and potential synergistic or competitive interactions that may be influenced by CQDs exposure (Fig. 7l). The microbiota importance points identified key OTUs that contributed most significantly to the observed changes in microbial community structure, which could help to pinpoint the most influential microbial species in CQDs-induced disturbances (Fig. 7m). In conclusion, CQDs remarkably induced pulmonary dysbacteriosis based on Aonis analysis and Anosim analysis (Table S3 and S4).

Given the significance of gut microbiota as the largest microbial community in organisms, it is imperative to ascertain whether CQDs, which have been observed to induce alterations in the pulmonary microbiome, exert similar effects on the gut microbiota. According to the microbial sequencing results, even though CQDs exposure, especially 5 mg/kg BW changed the feature of gut microflora in mice along with alterations in microbial diversity and composition across taxonomic levels (Figure S3 and S4), the statistical analysis suggested CQDs might not significantly disrupt the gut microbiome (Table S3 and S4).
Alterations of lung microbiome induced by CQDs caused nerve injury through regulating inflammatory responses across tissues in mice

In light of our observations that intranasal exposed CQDs affected the pulmonary microbiota and then elicited neurobehavioral impairments and neuronal death, the regulatory mechanisms underlying the lung-brain axis were further to be elucidated. Since inflammatory reactions to nanoparticles was one of commonly toxic mechanisms and closely related to the occurrence of ferroptosis and disulfidptosis, we firstly assessed the potential inflammatory responses in multiple organs of mice after CQDs. The pathological analysis highlighted the areas of inflammatory cell infiltration in tissues of cortex, hippocampus, lung and small intestine, with more pronounced inflammatory effects observed in high-dose CQDs group ( Fig. 8a∼c and S2c∼S2e), while no obvious inflammation relevant damages were observed in livers and colon (Figure S2f∼S2h).

We further clarified the inflammatory reaction to CQDs in brain and lung of mice. In the cortex and hippocampus of mice, the number and soma size of microglia was significantly elevated in the cortex and CA1 section of CQDs-exposed mice, while morphological changes of microglia including endpoints and process lengths revealed significant reduction in the cortex and CA1 section after CQDs treatment (Fig. 8d∼8i). The results indicated CQDs could activate microglia that are resident immune cells of the CNS and promoting neuroinflammatory process. Meanwhile, the inflammation in lung of mice treated with CQDs were evidenced by marked increases in immune cell counts (Fig. 8j and k) and levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-1ß, TNF-α and IL-6 (Fig. 8l∼n). More importantly, the contents of pro-inflammatory cytokines in serum (Fig. 8l∼n) and immune cells of white blood cells, lymphocyte and monocyte in blood (Table S6) were significantly higher in CQDs-exposed mice compared with the control, which underscored the systemic inflammation might involve in regulating the inflammation triggered by CQDs in both brain and lung through the lung-brain axis.

For a more in-depth evaluation, the association between disturbances of lung microbiota caused by respiratory exposed CQDs and the manifestation of inflammation, especially neuroinflammation were explored. According to the bioinformation analysis of lung microflora significantly shifted by CQDs, specific microbial functions emphasized the disruption of metabolic pathways related to glutathione and cysteine within the lung microbiota and links to inflammatory responses ( Fig. 9a∼c). Meanwhile, the correlation analysis provides a quantitative measure of the relationship between respiratory microbiota changes, particularly absolute abundance and Rikenellaceae and inflammation in lung, circulation system, and cortex and hippocampus of brain (Fig. 9d∼g). Moreover, the neuroinflammation were closely associated with glutathione and cysteine metabolic reprogramming that was involved in mediating ferroptosis and disulfidptosis in brain (Figure S5). The findings suggested that CQDs-induced alterations in the respiratory microbiota may contribute to lung inflammation and result in neuroinflammation to influence neurological health.

The intranasal instillation of CQDs in this study tried to simulates respiratory exposure, a method chosen based on reported studies that have shown this route to effectively model real-world exposure scenarios [7,30]. Even though the concentration of CQDs in the reality is challenging to be detected accurately because of their complex composition and dynamic transformation in environmental substrates, the dosages (5 mg/kg BW treated by around 20 µL/nose) selected here were determined by the internal exposure concentrations in living organisms of carbon-based nanoparticles being µg/L/kg/m3 in air, with coefficient 1000 as reported by some studies [31,32]. Given the low efficiency of nose-to-brain translocation, approximately 2 %, this higher dose that even significantly exceeds the OECD’s standard oral acute toxicity test maximum dose of 2 mg/kg ensures that a biologically significant amount of CQDs reaches the CNS. Therefore, the treatment administration is consistent with prolonged exposure to CQDs in order to assess their increasing risk to the public and improve their sustainable development.

Owning to the bright autofluorescence of CQDs, we visually observed that inhaled CQDs accumulated mainly in lung and digestive system but slightly in pericerebral parts of brain (Fig. 1 and S1) [33]. However, the findings, consistent with our previous research [9] and reported studies [10,11,34], indicated that the respiratory exposure to CQDs still could lead to neurobehavioral impairments that are closely associated with neuronal cell death including two novel types of ferroptosis and disulfidptosis in cortex and hippocampus (Fig. 2–5).

Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of cell death following with lipid peroxidation [25]. Disulfidptosis is a recently discovered form of regulated cell death mediated by the NADPH depletion and aberrant disulfide bonding in actin cytoskeleton proteins [28]. Our findings, including ferrous iron overload, lipid peroxidation and massive accumulation of intracellular disulfide molecules all precipitate these forms of cell death in the cortex and hippocampus of mice treated with CQDs. We next chose the AFADESI-MSI approach to explore the molecular pathways of intranasal treated CQDs mediating neuronal ferroptosis and disulfidptosis in specific regions of brain, i.e. cortex and hippocampus that both play critical roles in neurobehavioral regulation. The findings suggested that the disruption of the glutathione and cysteine metabolic pathways, which are pivotal in cellular redox balance, could involve in the mediation of CQDs triggering oxidative stress-induced cell death [25,29]. CQDs exposure reprogrammed this metabolic pathway by inducing GSH depletion and cysteine deficiency, which are critical in the pathogenesis of two types of programmed cell deaths called ferroptosis and disulfidptosis. Meanwhile, critical enzymes regulating GSH and cysteine contents, including SLC7A11, NADPH, and GPx4 were also observed to be affected by CQDs. Among these metabolic enzymes, the supply of NADPH is remarkably crucial for not only biosynthesizing cysteine and reducing disulfide bond but also maintaining the reduced state of GSH [28,29], which underlie the susceptibility of neurons to ferroptosis and disulfidptosis following CQDs exposure.

The nasal exposure to carbon-based nanomaterials, such as graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes, has been a subject of our investigation [22,23] or others [8], with studies indicating that their effects can vary significantly from those of metal nanomaterials. Taken silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) as an example, this kind of commonly used metal nanoparticles can elicit a robust inflammatory response and oxidative stress upon nasal exposure, which is usual route for nanoparticles to access the CNS [35]. In contrast, carbon-based nanomaterials have been observed to have varying degrees of biopersistence and potential for translocation to the brain, and resulting in distinct neurological effects [8], which presented a similar trend of neurotoxic effects of CQDs in this study. This comparison is essential for elucidating the nanomaterial-specific risks in the CNS, emphasizing the need for tailored safety assessments. Recently, more and more studies on nanoparticles have revealed their potential to alter gut microbial composition and function, which can subsequently impact host health. The mechanistic link between the gut microbiota and the CNS is complex and involves multiple pathways, including the vague nerve transmitting signals from the gut to the brain and the production of neuroactive compounds by gut microbes affecting brain function [16,17]. All in all, it is becoming increasingly clear that nanoparticles can have far-reaching effects on neurological health through their impact on the gut microbiota. In comparison, the role of lung microbiota in the context of nanoparticles exposure is less explored but gaining attention. The lung’s initial encounter with inhaled CQDs positions it as a potential gateway for effects on multiple systems, including CNS, but its regulatory role in the neurotoxicity of CQDs remains unclear. Lung microbiome has been increasingly recognized to modulate host neurological functions that is similar to gut microbiome-brain axis [14,15], which inspired us the probably mechanisms of CQDs impairing neurobehaviors through inducing dysbiosis in the lung microbiome. According to microbial sequencing analysis of BALF and feces of mice, respiratory exposure to CQDs indeed significantly altered the pulmonary, instead of intestinal microbiota, corroborated by limited available researches that reported similar effects of nanomaterials on lung microbial communities (Figs. 6 and 7) [18,36].

One of the primary mechanisms by which lung microbiota dysbiosis could affect neuronal death and neurobehavioral changes is through the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and chemokines [37,38]. These inflammatory mediators can enter the circulation to trigger a systemic inflammatory response, which has bene found to impact the brain [39]. The observed inflammatory responses in circulation system of mice exposed to CQDs could, in turn, initiate or exacerbate neuroinflammation through the activation of microglia and the release of pro-inflammatory mediators (Figs. 8 and 9). In accordance with our findings, the neuroinflammation environment has been found to further reprogram the glutathione and cysteine metabolic pathways, creating a vicious cycle that amplifies redox imbalance and disulfide stress that mediate cell death signals of ferroptosis and disulfidptosis [40,41]. This cycle could be a key driver of the neurobehavioral deficits of mice exposed to CQDs observed in our study.

However, there are still some limitations on this study, especially some potential confounding factors that may influence the results. One such factor is the stress associated with the nasal administration process itself, which could introduce variability in the mice models because the physical act of intranasal instillation may cause discomfort or distress to mice, leading to physiological changes that could be mistaken for neurological effects of CQDs exposure [42]. Additionally, the study did not fully account for individual variations in the baseline lung microbiota of mice, which could also influence the response to CQDs and the subsequent neurological effects. Addressing these limitations in future studies could provide a more comprehensive understanding of the true impact of CQDs on microbiome dynamics in respiratory tract and neurological health.

The crosstalk among the lung microbiome, systemic inflammation, and neuroinflammation thus emerges as a critical axis in the pathogenesis of CQDs-induced neurotoxicity [43]. While these mechanisms are promising, it is important to note that the field of microbiome-lung-brain axis, especially under environmental pollutants exposure is still in its infancy, and much remains to be understood. The complex interplay among the lung microbiome, systemic inflammation, neuroinflammation and neuronal death is likely to involve multiple but interconnected pathways. More importantly, the precise molecular mechanisms of CQDs on the microbial metabolites to induce systemic inflammation and affect neuroinflammation are warranted in order to develop potential therapeutic strategies to mitigate the neurological effects of lung microbiome disturbances. Based on this study, further research should focus on the bioaccumulation effects and chronic impacts on the nasal cavity’s microbiota and neurological health caused by long-term, low-dose exposure to CQDs, especially the mechanistic pathways of CQDs interactions with the lung microbiota and their neurological consequences, using advanced tracking and molecular biology techniques.

In this study, the respiratory exposure to CQDs results in neurobehavioral deficits potentially through the disruption of glutathione and cysteine metabolic pathways, leading to neuronal cell deaths of ferroptosis and disulfidptosis. The dysbiosis in lung other than gut microbiota was significantly induced by CQDs that is highly correlate with systemic and neuroinflammatory responses, which suggested a pivotal role of the microbiome-lung-brain axis in CQDs-induced neurotoxicity. These findings underscore the importance of considering the respiratory microbiome as a potential target and modulator of CQDs in the environment damaging the CNS, which provides novel insights into the precise prevention and control for the neurotoxic effects of CQDs and analogous nanomaterials.

Since carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are commercially produced in large quantities, their distribution in the environment and subsequent human exposure are inevitable. Inhalation is one of the primary modes of CQDs exposure in the environment, but the mechanisms of neurotoxic effects of CQDs following respiratory exposure are not yet clear. This study indicated a microbiome-lung-brain axis as a critical pathway may cause neuronal damage after respiratory exposure to CQDs, which provides insights on the understanding and managing the environmental and health risks associated with a novel environmental pollutant CQDs.
This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 82373617, 82103884).

Jing He: Formal analysis, Data curation. Pengcheng Xing: Validation, Data curation. Zongjian Ye: Formal analysis, Data curation. Jieyi Xia: Formal analysis, Data curation. Jiafu Yang: Validation, Data curation. Yijing Qian: Validation. Min Chen: Validation. Kehan Liu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Siyuan Chen: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tianshu Wu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2025.137255.
